title: Real vs. nominal GDP
description: Practice what you've learned about real GDP, nominal GDP, and the GDP deflator in this exercise.
topic_path: Economic indicators and the business cycle

assessment_items.question_content:
Justinia is a country that produces three goods: guitars, physics books, and sandals. 

**Which of the following would definitely cause an increase in nominal GDP but not a change real GDP in Justinia?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The quantity of goods produced stays the same; prices increase
The quantity of goods produced increases; prices increase
The quantity of goods produced increases; prices decrease
The quantity of goods produced decreases; prices stay the same
The quantity of goods produced increases; prices stay the same

assessment_items.question_content:
The nation of Asilia spent $\$200\text{ million}$ on goods and services in 2010.

In 2011 the country spent $\$220\text{ million}$ on goods and services, even though output decreased compared to 2010.

**What happened to real GDP and nominal GDP between these two years?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: real GDP increased; nominal GDP increased
real GDP decreased; nominal GDP decreased
real GDP increased; nominal GDP decreased
real GDP is unchanged; nominal GDP increased
real GDP decreased; nominal GDP increased

assessment_items.question_content:
Suppose the amount of output doesn’t change in an economy, but the consumer price index (CPI) increases.

**What happens to nominal gross domestic product (GDP) and real GDP?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Nominal GDP increases; real GDP doesn’t change.
Nominal GDP increases; real GDP increases.
Nominal GDP increases; real GDP decreases.
Nominal GDP decreases; real GDP decreases.
Nominal GDP doesn’t change; real GDP increases.

assessment_items.question_content:
Good | Quantity produced in 2016 | Price in 2016 | Quantity produced in 2017 | Price in 2017
-|-|-|-|-
Guitars | $20$ | $\$50$ | $20$ | $\$75$
Physics books | $100$ |$\$55$ | $100$ | $\$60$
Sandals | $100$ | $\$20$ | $100$ | $\$25$

Production and prices in the nation of Justinia in 2016 and 2017 are shown in the table above.

**Which of the following best describes what happened to real  GDP and nominal GDP between 2016 and 2017?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Real GDP increased; nominal GDP increased
Real GDP increased; nominal GDP decreased
Real GDP has not changed; nominal GDP increased
Real GDP has not changed; nominal GDP has not changed
Real GDP increased; Nominal GDP has decreased

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the distinction between real GDP and nominal GDP?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Nominal GDP uses current prices; real GDP uses constant prices
Nominal GDP includes intermediate goods; real GDP excludes intermediate goods
Nominal GDP is calculated every year; real GDP is calculated only occasionally
Nominal GDP is adjusted for changes in prices; real GDP is not adjusted for changes in prices
Nominal GDP is calculated by deflating real GDP; real GDP is unadjusted for inflation

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the nominal GDP and the real GDP for the country of Hamsterville over three years

Period | Nominal GDP | Real GDP
- | - | -
Year $1$ | $\$120 \text{ billion}$ | $\$130\text{ billion}$
Year $2$ | $\$150\text{ billion}$ | $\$150\text{ billion}$
Year $3$ | $\$160\text{ billion}$ | $\$140\text{ billion}$

**Which of the following can be concluded from the data above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Year $1$ is the base year for the GDP deflator price index
The economy's output of goods and services increased every year between years $1$ and $3$
The composition of the country's output changed between years $2$ and $3$, shifting from capital goods to consumer goods
The economy experienced inflation between years $2$ and $3$
The economy experienced deflation between years $1$ and $2$

assessment_items.question_content:
The country of Neverland produces two goods: puppy shampoo and butter sculptures. The table below shows the output and prices of the two goods over three years:

Time period | Output of puppy shampoo | Price of puppy shampoo | Output of butter sculptures | Price of butter sculptures 
- | - | - | - | - 
Year $1$ | $8$ | $\$3$ | $25$ | $\$5$
Year $2$ | $10$ | $\$5$ | $30$ | $\$6$
Year $3$ | $12$ | $\$4$ | $25$ | $\$6$

**What is Neverland's real gross domestic product (GDP) in Year $2$, assuming Year $1$ is the base year?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Real GDP measures the value of a nation's output in one year in constant prices from a base year
To find real GDP you must multiply the output of all goods in one year by the prices in the base year and add the results together

Neverland's real GDP for year $2$ can be calculated as:

(Output of puppy shampoo $\times$ the price of puppy shampoo in the base year) $+$ (Output of butter sculptures $\times$ price of butter sculptures in the base year)

Neverland's nominal GDP for year $2$ is:

$(14 \times \$3) + (30 \times \$5) = \$42+\$150 = \$192$

assessment_items.question_content:
The country of Neverland produces two goods: puppy shampoo and butter sculptures. The table below shows the output and prices of the two goods over three years:

Time period | Output of puppy shampoo | Price of puppy shampoo | Output of butter sculptures | Price of butter sculptures 
- | - | - | - | - 
Year $1$ | $8$ | $\orange{\$3}$ | $25$ | $\orange{\$5}$
Year $2$ | $10$ | $\blueD{\$5}$ | $30$ | $\blueD{\$6}$
Year $3$ | $12$ | $\orange{\$4}$ | $25$ | $\orange{\$6}$

Neverland calculates real GDP each year using constant prices, and year 2 is the base year.

**What is the value of real GDP in year 3, using year 2's prices?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To calculate real GDP using constant prices, calculate the value of production in each year using the base years' prices.

Time period | Output of puppy shampoo | Price of puppy shampoo | Output of butter sculptures | Price of butter sculptures | Value of real GDP
- | - | - | - | - 
Year $1$ | $8$ | $\$3$ | $25$ | $\$5$ | $(8\times\blueD{\$5})+(25\times\blueD{$6})=\$190$
Year $2$ | $10$ | $\blueD{\$5}$ | $30$ | $\blueD{\$6}$ | $(10\times\blueD{\$5})+(30\times\blueD{$6})=\$220$
Year $3$ | $12$ | $\$4$ | $25$ | $\$6$ | $(12\times\blueD{\$5})+(25\times\blueD{$6})=\$210$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is one potential problem that may result from calculating real GDP using constant prices of products in a base year?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When the prices of some goods fall over time, calculating their value in constant prices makes these goods seem like a larger share of GDP than they really are
When prices of all goods rise over time, calculating their value in constant prices can understate how much real output increased between years
When the prices of all goods decrease over time, calculating the value of output in constant prices can overstate how much real output increased between years
When prices do not change over time, the real GDP will not change either
When prices decrease and output decreases over time, real GDP will decrease as well

assessment_items.question_content:
The Center for Economic Research in Frankland has compiled the macroeconomic data in the table below:

Economic indicator | Value
-|-
Nominal GDP in 2018 | $\$500$ million
Real GDP in 2018 | $\$400$ million
Cost of a market basket of goods in 2018 | $\$1200$
Cost of a market basket of goods in the base year | $\$800$

**Calculate the GDP deflator in 2018 and the CPI in 2018.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: You need to calculate nominal GDP in each year to calculate the deflator.

choices.content:
$\begin{align} \text{GDP deflator} & =125 \\\\
\text{CPI}&=150 \end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{GDP deflator} & = 125\\\\
\text{CPI}&=50\% \end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{GDP deflator} & =80 \\\\
\text{CPI}&= 66.67\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{GDP deflator} & =100 \\\\
\text{CPI}&= 400\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{GDP deflator} & = 41.67 \\\\
\text{CPI}&= 50 \end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows nominal GDP (in billions of dollars) and the GDP deflator for the country of Chucknorristan over several decades.

Year | Nominal GDP (billions) | GDP deflator
- | - | -
1970 | $\$20$ | $50$
1980 | $\$32$ | $80$
1990 | $\$45$ | $100$
2000 | $\$80$ | $160$
2010 |  $\$120$ |  $250$

**What was real GDP in Chucknorristan in 1980?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To find real GDP you must adjust the nominal GDP figures to their real values using the GDP deflator.

To calculate real GDP using a GDP deflator, first divide the deflator by 100, and then divide nominal GDP by that value:

 $\begin{align} \text{ convert GDP deflator} &=\dfrac{\text{deflator}}{100}\\\\
\text{real GDP}_{1980}&=\dfrac{\text{nominal GDP}_{1980}}{\text{converted deflator}_{1980}}\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{ convert GDP deflator} &=\dfrac{\text{deflator}}{100}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{80}{100}\\
\\
\text{real GDP}_{1980}&=\dfrac{\text{nominal GDP}_{1980}}{\text{converted deflator}_{1980}}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{\$32\text{ billion}}{0.8}\\
\\
&=\$40\text{ billion}\end{align}$

choices.content: $\$32$ billion
$\$40$ billion
$\$80$ billion
$\$400$ million
$\$2.5$ billion

assessment_items.question_content:
In 2017 Spamland's real GDP was $\$50\text{ billion}$ and the GDP deflator was $120$. 

**What was Spamland's nominal GDP in 2017?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $\$20\text{ billion}$
$\$41.6\text{ billion}$
$\$60\text{ billion}$
$\$6,000\text{ billion}$
$\$0.416\text{ billion}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The nation of Cuddlevia manufactures just two goods: short-sleeved snuggies and body pillows. The table below shows the output and prices of these two goods over several years.

Time period | Output of short-sleeved snuggies | Price of short-sleeved snuggies | Output of body pillows | Price of body pillows 
- | - | - | - | - 
$2016$ | $40$ | $\$8$ | $22$ | $\$16$
$2017$ | $50$ | $\$10$ | $25$ | $\$20$
$2018$ | $44$ | $\$9$ | $32$ | $\$20$

**What is the GDP deflator price index for 2017 using 2016 as a base year?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The GDP deflator is the nominal GDP divided by the real GDP times $100$:

$GDP \text{ deflator}=\dfrac{\text{nominal }GDP}{\text{real }GDP}\times 100$

To determine Cuddlevia's nominal GDP you must calculate the value of 2017's output in current (2017) prices:

Time period | Output of short-sleeved snuggies | Price of short-sleeved snuggies | Output of body pillows | Price of body pillows 
- | - | - | - | - 
$2016$ | $40$ | $\$8$ | $22$ | $\$16$
$2017$ | $\maroonD{50}$ | $\maroonD{\$10}$ | $\maroonD{25}$ | $\maroonD{\$20}$

$\begin{align}\text {Nominal }GDP_{2017}&= (\maroonD{50} \times \maroonD{\$10}) + (\maroonD{25} \times \maroonD{\$20}) \\\\&= \maroonD{\$1,000}\end{align}$

To determine Cuddlevia's real GDP you must calculate the value of 2017's output in constant (2016) prices: 

Time period | Output of short-sleeved snuggies | Price of short-sleeved snuggies | Output of body pillows | Price of body pillows 
- | - | - | - | - 
$2016$ | $40$ | $\blueD{\$8}$ | $22$ | $\blueD{\$16}$
$2017$ | $\blueD{50}$ | $\$10$ | $\blueD{25}$ | $\$20$


$\begin{align}\text {Real }GDP_{2017}&= (\blueD{50} \times \blueD{\$8}) + (\blueD{25} \times \blueD{\$16}) \\\\&= \blueD{\$800}\end{align}$

Once you've determined nominal and real GDP, the deflator can be calculated:

$GDP\text{ deflator}_{2017}= \dfrac{\maroonD{\text{Nominal }GDP_{2017}}}{\blueD{\text{Real }GDP_{2017}}}\times100$

Using the values we calculated for nominal and real GDP:

$\begin{align}\text{GDP Deflator}&=\dfrac{\maroonD{\text{Nominal GDP}_{2017}}}{\blueD{\text{Real GDP}_{2017}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{1000}}{\maroonD{800}}\times 100\\\\
&=1.25\times 100\\\\
&=125\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Key macroeconomic data for Hamsterville is given in the table below

Economic indicator | Value
-|-
Real GDP in 2019 | $\$20$ million
Nominal GDP in 2019 | $\$60$ million
Cost of a market basket of goods in 2019 | $\$160$
Cost of a market basket of goods in the base year | $\$100$

**What is Hamsterville’s GDP deflator for 2019?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To calculate the GDP deflator, divide nominal GDP by real GDP, then multiply by 100:

$\text{GDP deflator} = \dfrac{\text{Nominal GDP}}{\text{Real GDP}}\times 100$
Using the values in the table:

$\begin{align} \text{GDP deflator} &= \dfrac{\text{Nominal GDP}}{\text{Real GDP}}\times 100\\\\
&=\dfrac{60}{20}\times100\end{align}$
The GDP deflator is $300$:

$\begin{align} \text{GDP deflator} &= \dfrac{\text{Nominal GDP}}{\text{Real GDP}}\times 100\\\\
&=\dfrac{60}{20}\times100\\\\
&=3 \times 100\\\\
&=300\end{align}$
